title: Traveling with Kyle - Luxemburg and France

transcript.text: Okay, Luxembourg, so I wrote from Belgium into Luxembourg, I noticed that Luxembourg was like all clear cut. I look over in every direction, and there's like huge patches of clear cut, just totally flat.

And then they'd have like a patch of trees and then clear cut, and it was kind of hilly, but it was kind of like, oh, these people just destroyed their country. And then I went into Luxembourg's capital city, which actually is really beautiful. I rode in, there was some guy like on a road bike, and I was drafting off him going really fast. And by that time, I was starting to get really strong, which was cool. It's awesome to be really strong. That's one of the best things about bike touring, is you get really strong.

So I was like drafting off of him, and then I rode up some hill, and I saw some huge bridge that just spans across some huge like cavernous ravine. As I go up the hill, and I guess Town Center was like on top of the hill, maybe next to like the central capital town, was like their castle on the top of the town.
And then I went in like some pedestrian mall, that was kind of like where that bridge like went, bridged.
And yeah, you have to pay for the bathrooms, though. I think that's maybe where I first noticed that. You have to pay to go to the bathroom in Europe. It's kind of sucks.
And yeah, those looks of Bergens, though, they were kind of grumpy. They didn't seem like they were having a whole lot of fun.

And then I went outside of that town, and I camped out in some like forest. And I think it was like a brand newly planted forest or something, because I think, yeah, all the trees were like, they were big, but they're all in like rows.
And between the trees, it was totally flat. So that was cool. Because in England, when I was camping, and I, you know, I'd have to deal with like thorns, there's tons of thorns in England.
So I'd have to like duck in and go under, like wiggle like a worm underneath and some like, what do you call it, ditch to sleep and be careful about the thorns.
So that was cool to be in that little area. And then I went into France the next day. And I was excited to go into France initially, because I took French in college for one semester.
And I thought, I thought the French were like cool and stuff. But I went into some town and like, I was like tired one day, and I only rode 17 miles.

So I just decided to, and it was like the first day of not riding all day long. So I decided to just like, I got a coffee and I put my foot up outside and some like guys like, Oh, no, don't put your foot up on the tape on the chair.
So I thought it was kind of weird. And then he's like, you buy another coffee. And after like a half hour being there, and I was like, but I just got one. He's like, you have to get another one.
And I was like, okay, so I think I've left. And then I rode down and I was like going up hills and it was pretty.
And I remember at one point I got like an outside for like hot dog. But and one night I slept like under a bridge in France.

But I was riding along for Anson, just riding all day long. And but one thing I until I got to Paris, and one night I slept like right on the highway, like between like where the exit is on the grass to the exit is and like an overpass on another highway, where cars could see me, but I guess they'd have to look over to see me.

So like when you camp out, you sometimes you can camp out in broad sight, be in get away with it because people aren't going to be looking over, you know, because even though they look if they look and they see you.
But uh, they don't look over so. Um, one kind of interesting thing I noticed about France, like I go into like a bar once and like some kid he like, he like kissed his son on the lips.
That was kind of interesting. They were like really touchy feely there. And that was in another bar and some guy came in the bar and he shook everybody's hand in the bar, including mine.
So, that was kind of interesting. Um, and kind of like they know everybody and they just have to, I don't know, that's kind of cool, friendly.
And um, but I kind of got the, got a kind of a weird vibe off of like those rural people. It seems like up in their mouth, they do this mouth thing like this.
So it kind of made me a little bit paranoid. Then I went into Paris and I was trying to ride my bike into Paris, but there was no way to get into Paris.
It was just highways. And I, uh, I'm riding down Paris and some cop, the highway and some cop is like, Oh, you can't do this.
So he actually escorted me. He drove in front of me and to some exit. He said, okay, get off here and go to the, uh, go over to ride in, I guess the subway.
And so I kept asking all these people, I'm like, how do I get into the center of Paris? And they're like, they're like, Oh, just take the subway.
And after enough people were like, yeah, take the subway. I was like, okay, yeah. Why don't I just take the subway? Originally I didn't want to cause it would cost money.
But then I was like, why not? So I just brought my bike in and put it on the subway, which is awesome.
I think most countries, Korea, they let you do that. But like in Chile, they'll freak. They won't let you bring your bike in the subway.
I've done it like once, but no, so that was really cool, but it was the only way to get my bike in there.
So I got my bike in and then I locked it there and I did the Champs-Élysées tower thing.
And then I did the Eiffel tower, which took like three and a half hours to get up to the top of it.
It's a total tourist trap, but I'm glad I did it because I wanted to, uh, have that experience, I guess, like everybody else there.
And then I took the photos of it. Paris is a pretty town, just lots of, um, whitewashed colored buildings everywhere.
And I looked north and I saw some building that looked like this. That was interesting.
I didn't go into this, to the, um, that part of downtown though.
And I went into some McDonald's right there on the Champs-Élysées, which is like the biggest McDonald's I've ever seen in my life.
It was like two stories, huge.
And it had like seven different lines that were like 30 feet long each of people.
Like, uh, it has lots of business there.
But, um, it seemed like a cool place.
I was only there one night though.
Well, maybe I'll go back and check out the Louvre and all that stuff.

So that night I got on the bus, on the, uh, Paris, on the train and I saw, like, they have, like, a little bunny rabbit, like, to, as, like, the person to show you how to put your hand in the door and stuff.
I thought that was kind of cute. We don't have bunny rabbits representing people in America.
But I, I took the train all the way down to the very last stop on the south, got off it, and by then I was pretty much out of the town.
Like, they have a really great metro system there.
And I rode my bike up a hill and I just rode up off the side of the road and I slept on some hill on school.
I could have gotten, I could have, I should have hung out in Paris for a week because I could have been camping out the whole time.
By just taking the subway out of town.
But I, uh, yeah, I got on my bike and I rode down south through these towns and the whole part of, like, from Luxembourg to Paris to Spain, the whole time was just flat.
France is flat like Kansas.
I didn't see any mountains.
It was crazy. Totally flat. Not one hill.
It was crazy.
But I rode through and I saw a couple of those bullet trains go by.
That was kind of a culture shock because they don't go that fast in America.
It was in, like, 150 miles an hour.
And so I rode down south and I'd see some, like, little towns, which was kind of cute.
You know, little, little, little buildings.
And one thing I was noticing about Europe is there's, like, grass that grows out of the buildings.
Because we don't have, in Colorado we don't have buildings that are old enough that actually have grass and trees growing out of them.
That was interesting.
And, uh, yeah, so I rode down the, down the roads and I was starting to get pretty strong by then.
I think I was going, like, 140 miles a day.
And, um, um, I was eating at McDonald's the whole time, every single night.
Because there were lots of McDonald's in France.
And I, and so whenever I saw McDonald's I'd go there and I'd get two Happy Meals.
I'd get two number ones.
And, uh, that's what I'd have for dinner.
And it was delicious.
Kept me going.
And, uh, and I'd, like, go in the bathroom and I'd take off my spandex suit.
I don't know, that's what I would sleep in.
I would, like, I'd just, like, you know, I guess get toilet paper.
Like, I think I had a rag and some soap.
And I would just, like, like, wipe myself down on my arms and my legs.
And that was, like, my armpit.
And that was it.
That was the shower I'd have for a whole week.
That was what I was doing for, like, getting myself clean.
And, um, so, yeah, I just sleep in the trees just like in England.
And France has lots of, um, lots of, uh, um, camping.
I mean, lots of, uh, thorns, too.
Thorn problems.
We don't have thorns in Colorado.
Um, so I kind of had to be careful about that when I'm going off the road to sleep.
Um, but sometimes I would just go off and there would be, like, a cool, um,
Forest.
And France, it seemed to me, was a lot less popular than England.
Um, I didn't have as much trouble.
I'd have more opportunity to walk through forests.
And, um, but, like, and then, uh, yeah.
Then I went into the Pyrenees.
And, um, so that's, I guess, my Spanish story.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
There's a couple of guys here.
All right.
Okay.
All right.
Okay.

Thank you.
